Alex Morgan: Welcome back to The Long View — Sustainability. Today we're talking about macroeconomics, and I'm joined by Skanda Eminas, macro economist. Skanda Eminas, thanks for being here.
Skanda Eminas: Thanks, Alex Morgan. Great to be back.
Alex Morgan: Today's episode is sponsored by Bloomberg. Bloomberg Terminal is the data spine that institutional desks run on. Try bloomberg.com today.Before we get into it — Dr. Elena Fisher was on last week talking about systems thinking.
Skanda Eminas: Yeah, exactly. And it ties into what we're covering today.
Alex Morgan: I want to flag something — Dr. Elena Fisher mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.I want to flag something — Dr. Eliana Fishler mentioned this same dynamic a couple episodes back.Let's start there. What's the structural angle most listeners don't have access to?
Skanda Eminas: Honestly, Employment data is noisier than headlines suggest. We've seen this play out at teams using IEA. Once you internalize that, the rest of the decisions get easier.
Alex Morgan: Take me through the decision. Where do systems thinking and macroeconomics intersect?
Skanda Eminas: Honestly, Systems thinking in macro means watching feedback loops, not point estimates. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Alex Morgan: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Skanda Eminas: Here's how I think about it. Risk management at the central-bank level looks like reliability engineering. We've seen this play out at teams using IPCC. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Alex Morgan: That maps to what I've seen.
Skanda Eminas: Outside of Project Drawdown-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Alex Morgan: We'll be right back after a quick word from our sponsors.

Alex Morgan: I want to dig into that. Where do systems thinking and macroeconomics intersect?
Skanda Eminas: It comes down to this: Systems thinking in macro means watching feedback loops, not point estimates. If you ignore that, you end up rebuilding it from scratch a year later.
Alex Morgan: What does the failure mode look like? What's the most underrated piece of macroeconomics?
Skanda Eminas: Here's how I think about it. Risk management at the central-bank level looks like reliability engineering. We've seen this play out at teams using IEA. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Alex Morgan: That maps to what I've seen.
Skanda Eminas: And when teams adopt Project Drawdown in that flow, the trade-off becomes visible — The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Alex Morgan: Let's pivot — risk management is where I want to spend the last stretch.
Skanda Eminas: That's where the conversation gets interesting, because the trade-offs around risk management cross-cut everything we've covered.
Alex Morgan: Walk me through a concrete example. What's the version of the question that actually matters?
Skanda Eminas: Sure. Employment data is noisier than headlines suggest. We've seen this play out at teams using IEA. And the second-order effect is the part most people miss.
Alex Morgan: What's the one heuristic you keep coming back to? What's something you used to believe about this that you've revised?
Skanda Eminas: Here's how I think about it. Systems thinking in macro means watching feedback loops, not point estimates. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Alex Morgan: That's a useful framing. What's the most underrated piece of macroeconomics?
Skanda Eminas: Yeah, so — Risk management at the central-bank level looks like reliability engineering. We've seen this play out at teams using IPCC. The framing matters because it tells you which trade-offs you're making.
Alex Morgan: Where does that break down?
Skanda Eminas: Outside of Carbon Trust-class tooling, the failure mode is harder to spot. I've watched this pattern hold across three different teams.
Alex Morgan: Before we wrap, what's one thing listeners can try this week?
Skanda Eminas: Pick one of the ideas we covered and run it for two weeks. The rest is iteration.
